title: Group Bookings at Fora: Complete Guide
body:
Published: January 15, 2025
Last Updated: January 2, 2026
Status:  Source of Truth
WHAT IS A GROUP BOOKING?
A group booking is when you're arranging travel for multiple people traveling together. At Fora, we define groups
by the number of rooms, cabins, or travelers depending on the travel type:
— Hotels: 10+ rooms
— Cruises: 8+ staterooms
— Tours: 10+ travelers
Group bookings follow different processes than individual (FIT) bookings and typically involve contracts, deposits,
and special terms.
WHY BOOK GROUPS?
Benefits for advisors:
— Higher overall commission (commission on entire group vs. individual bookings)
— Simplified logistics (one contract vs. multiple individual reservations)
— Stronger client relationships (handling complex needs)
— Economies of scale
Benefits for clients:
— Group rates (often lower than individual rates)
— Room/cabin blocks held together
— Customized experiences
— Dedicated support from suppliers
IMPORTANT DIFFERENCES FROM FIT BOOKINGS
What changes with group bookings:
1. No preferred partner perks: Group bookings do not offer preferred partner perks and amenities
2. Contract required: Most groups require a formal contract with terms
3. Deposits and attrition: Groups typically require upfront deposits and have attrition clauses
4. Cut-off dates: Room blocks must be released by a specific date
5. Commission structure: May differ from FIT rates
TYPES OF GROUP BOOKINGS
Hotels
Minimum: 10+ rooms
Common group types:
— Weddings
— Corporate meetings
— Reunions
— Incentive travel
Key terms:
— Run of House (ROH): Group receives room types based on availability
— Room block: Specific number of rooms held for the group
— Attrition: Minimum number of rooms that must be filled (typically 20-30% below block size)
— Cut-off date: Deadline to release unused rooms (typically 30-60 days before arrival)
Cruises
Minimum: 8+ staterooms
Booking types:
— Speculative: Flexible hold, no deposit required initially
— Contracted: Formal commitment with deposit
— Charter: Full or partial ship buyout
Cruise-specific benefits:
— Tour Conductor (TC) Credits: Earned based on group size, typically 1 per 8-10 cabins
— Group Amenity Points (GAP): Can be exchanged for onboard credit, events, amenities
Tours
Minimum: 10+ travelers
Common scenarios:
— Custom group itineraries
— Special interest groups
— Educational travel
— Milestone celebrations
COMMISSION STRUCTURE
Group bookings typically earn commission based on total group revenue. Standard rates:
— Hotels (major chains): 7%
— Hotels (boutique/luxury): 10-12%
— Cruises: ~16%
— Tour operators: 16-18%
— Wholesalers: ~15%
Note: Rates may vary by supplier and group size.
KEY CONTRACT TERMS
Attrition
The minimum number of rooms/cabins the group must fill. Industry standard is 20-30% attrition (meaning the
group must fill 70-80% of the block).
Example:
— Block of 50 rooms
— 20% attrition
— Group must fill at least 40 rooms or pay penalty
Cut-off Date
The date by which the group must finalize their room count. Unused rooms are released back to the hotel. Typically
30-60 days before arrival.
Deposit Schedule
Groups usually require deposits at booking and additional payments at intervals before arrival.
Complimentary Rooms
Hotels often provide complimentary rooms based on group size. Standard ratio is 1:40 (one comp room per 40
paid rooms).
GROUP BOOKING PROCESS
1. Initial inquiry: Gather group details (size, dates, budget, purpose)
2. RFP (Request for Proposal): Submit formal request to suppliers
3. Review proposals: Compare rates, terms, amenities
4. Contract negotiation: Work with supplier on terms
5. Contract signature: Formalize agreement
6. Deposit payment: Submit initial deposit
7. Manage rooming list: Collect traveler details and preferences
8. Cut-off date management: Finalize room count before deadline
9. Final payment: Submit remaining balance
10. Pre-arrival support: Coordinate details with supplier
11. Post-trip follow-up: Gather feedback, handle issues
WHEN TO INVOLVE FORA TEAMS
— Booking Platform team: Contract review, supplier negotiations, unusual requests
— Tour Ops team: Destination-specific guidance (especially Japan, Africa), custom itineraries
— Support team: Escalations, client issues during travel
RESOURCES
— Contract templates available in Portal
— Group booking intake form
— Supplier contact list for group sales
WHAT THIS GUIDE DOESN'T COVER
This guide provides foundational knowledge for group bookings at Fora. For detailed operational guidance on
specific scenarios, contract negotiations, or supplier-specific policies, consult with Booking Platform or Tour Ops
teams.

title: Getting Started with Group Bookings
body:
Published: March 14, 2023
Category: Group Bookings > Getting Started
WHAT QUALIFIES AS A GROUP?
Group travel is when you're booking for multiple people traveling together. Here's when bookings qualify for group
rates:
Hotels: 8+ rooms
Cruises: 5+ cabins
Tours: 10+ travelers
Once you reach these minimums, you'll work with the supplier's group sales team instead of booking individual
reservations.
WHY BOOK GROUPS?
For you:
— Higher commission on total group value
— Easier to manage one contract vs. many individual bookings
— Build expertise in a lucrative niche
For clients:
— Better rates
— Rooms/cabins together
— Special amenities
— Dedicated support
TYPES OF GROUPS
Leisure Groups
— Weddings
— Family reunions
— Milestone birthdays
— Travel clubs
Corporate Groups
— Meetings and conferences
— Incentive trips
— Team retreats
BASIC GROUP PROCESS
1. Inquiry: Client reaches out about group travel
2. Gather details: Size, dates, budget, purpose
3. Request quotes: Contact suppliers for group proposals
4. Present options: Review with client
5. Contract: Sign agreement with selected supplier
6. Deposit: Submit initial payment
7. Manage bookings: Collect traveler info and preferences
8. Finalize: Submit rooming list by cut-off date
9. Support: Coordinate details before and during travel
KEY TERMS TO KNOW
Room Block: Number of rooms reserved for your group
Attrition: Minimum rooms you must fill (typically 15-20% below block size)
Cut-off Date: Deadline to release unused rooms
Run of House: Hotel assigns room types based on availability
Rooming List: Document with traveler names, room preferences, special requests
GETTING STARTED TIPS
1. Start small: Begin with groups of 8-15 people
2. Use templates: Fora provides group intake forms and contract templates
3. Build relationships: Connect with hotel and cruise line group sales teams
4. Set expectations: Be clear with clients about deadlines and deposits
COMMON MISTAKES TO AVOID
— Not reading the contract carefully
— Missing cut-off dates
— Underestimating group size
— Forgetting to factor in attrition risk
— Not explaining cancellation policies to clients
WHEN TO ESCALATE
Reach out to Booking Platform team for:
— Groups over 50 rooms/cabins
— Complex contract terms
— Multi-property groups
— International destinations you're unfamiliar with
RESOURCES
— Group intake form: Available in Portal
— Contract templates: Available in Portal
— Supplier group contacts: Available in Portal
NEXT STEPS
Ready to dive deeper? Check out these articles:
— Understanding Hotel Group Contracts
— Cruise Group Booking Types
— Commission Structures for Group Travel
— Negotiating Group Terms

title: Understanding Hotel Group Contracts
body:
Published: January 8, 2026
Category: Group Bookings > Hotels
OVERVIEW
When booking 10+ hotel rooms, you'll typically work with the hotel's group sales team to negotiate a contract. This
guide covers the key terms you'll encounter and what to look for during contract review.
ESSENTIAL CONTRACT TERMS
Run of House (ROH)
The hotel assigns room types based on availability at check-in. Your group doesn't get to choose specific room
categories, but the hotel ensures appropriate accommodations.
When to accept ROH:
— Flexible group with mixed preferences
— Budget-conscious clients
— Lower rates offered in exchange
When to negotiate specific room types:
— Wedding blocks (consistency matters)
— VIP/executive groups
— Groups with accessibility needs
Attrition Clause
The minimum percentage of your room block that must be filled. If the group doesn't meet this threshold, you may
owe payment for unfilled rooms.
Standard attrition: 20-30% (meaning group must fill 70-80% of block)
Example:
— 40-room block
— 25% attrition
— Group must fill at least 30 rooms
— If only 28 rooms fill, client pays for 2 additional rooms
Negotiation tip: Higher attrition protects the client but may result in higher rates. Lower attrition gets better pricing
but increases risk.
Cut-off Date
The deadline to finalize your rooming list and release unused rooms back to the hotel. Typically 30-60 days before
arrival.
After cut-off:
— Unused rooms are released
— Group is charged for rooms up to attrition minimum
— Hotel can resell released inventory
Pro tip: Set internal cut-off 7-10 days before hotel deadline to give yourself buffer.
Deposit Schedule
Most hotel group contracts require:
1. Initial deposit: 10-25% at contract signing
2. Second deposit: 30-60 days before arrival
3. Final payment: At cut-off date or 14-30 days before arrival
Deposit amounts and timing are negotiable based on group size and hotel policy.
Complimentary Rooms
Hotels provide free rooms based on group size. Standard ratio: 1:40 (one comp room per 40 paid rooms).
Example:
— 80 paid rooms = 2 comp rooms
— 45 paid rooms = 1 comp room
— 35 paid rooms = 0 comp rooms
Comp rooms can be used for group organizer, tour manager, or sold to reduce client costs.
Sliding Scale Cancellation Policy
Cancellation penalties increase as arrival date approaches.
Typical structure:
— 90+ days out: Full refund minus deposit
— 60-89 days: 25% penalty
— 30-59 days: 50% penalty
— 0-29 days: 100% penalty (full payment due)
Always review cancellation terms carefully and explain them clearly to clients.
NEGOTIABLE ITEMS
What you can typically negotiate:
Rates:
— Base room rate
— Discounts for larger groups
— Shoulder date flexibility (slightly different dates for better rates)
Amenities:
— Complimentary breakfast
— Welcome reception
— Room upgrades
— Late checkout
— Resort fee waivers
Terms:
— Attrition percentage
— Cut-off date extension
— Deposit schedule adjustments
— Flexible cancellation terms
Meeting Space:
— Complimentary meeting rooms
— AV equipment
— Wi-Fi access
WHAT'S USUALLY NON-NEGOTIABLE
— Service charges and taxes
— Parking fees (sometimes)
— Resort fees at resort properties
— Minimum length of stay during peak periods
CONTRACT REVIEW CHECKLIST
Before signing, verify:
— [ ] Room block size matches your needs
— [ ] Rates are competitive and locked in
— [ ] Attrition clause is reasonable (20-30% range)
— [ ] Cut-off date gives adequate time
— [ ] Deposit schedule is manageable for client
— [ ] Cancellation policy is clear
— [ ] Complimentary room ratio is included (1:40)
— [ ] Any promised amenities are in writing
— [ ] Force majeure clause protects against unforeseen events
— [ ] Walking policy is defined (what happens if hotel oversells)
RED FLAGS
Watch out for:
— Attrition below 15% or above 35%: Too risky or too expensive
— Cut-off date less than 30 days: Not enough time to fill block
— No force majeure clause: Group at risk if event cancelled for legitimate reasons
— Unclear walking policy: What if hotel can't accommodate your group?
— Service charges not itemized: Hidden costs can surprise clients
WHEN TO ESCALATE TO BOOKING PLATFORM
Reach out to BP team for:
— Unfavorable contract terms you can't negotiate
— Multi-property or multi-destination groups
— Groups over 100 rooms
— International group bookings
— Complex F&B minimums or meeting space needs
— Legal questions about contract language
ADDITIONAL RESOURCES
— Contract templates: Available in Fora Portal
— Group intake form: Collect client requirements upfront

title: Attrition Clauses: Best Practices
body:
Published: April 19, 2023
Category: Group Bookings > Hotel Contracts
WHAT IS ATTRITION?
Attrition is the minimum percentage of your room block that must be filled. If your group doesn't meet this
threshold, you (or your client) pay penalties for the shortfall.
HOW ATTRITION WORKS
Example:
— You book a block of 30 rooms
— Contract has 15% attrition
— You must fill at least 26 rooms (30 - 15% = 26 rooms minimum)
— If you only fill 24 rooms, you owe payment for 2 additional rooms
STANDARD ATTRITION RATES
Industry standard: 15-20% attrition
Most hotels will offer attrition in this range. The lower the attrition percentage, the more rooms you must fill.
What different attrition rates mean:
— 10% attrition = must fill 90% of block (higher risk)
— 15% attrition = must fill 85% of block (recommended)
— 20% attrition = must fill 80% of block (more conservative)
— 25% attrition = must fill 75% of block (lower risk, but may cost more)
RECOMMENDED ATTRITION BY GROUP TYPE
Weddings
Recommended: 15% attrition
Weddings usually have firm headcounts. Guest lists are finalized months in advance, and attendance is predictable.
Corporate Groups
Recommended: 15-18% attrition
Corporate groups typically have confirmed attendees, but last-minute changes can happen (business needs,
schedule conflicts).
Reunions and Social Groups
Recommended: 18-20% attrition
Social groups have more uncertainty. People may drop out closer to the event date.
Incentive Groups
Recommended: 12-15% attrition
Incentive travel groups are usually fully funded by the company, so attendance is high.
Conferences
Recommended: 15-18% attrition
Conferences depend on registration success. Build in some buffer.
NEGOTIATING ATTRITION
Push for lower attrition (higher fill requirement) when:
— You're confident about group size
— Group has high commitment (deposits already collected)
— Peak season (hotel wants the business locked in)
Lower attrition = Better rates
Hotels offer lower pricing when you take on more risk.
Push for higher attrition (lower fill requirement) when:
— Group size is uncertain
— Event is far out (12+ months)
— Client hasn't collected deposits yet
— Shoulder season or low demand period
Higher attrition = More protection, but may cost more
RED FLAGS
Attrition below 10%: Too risky. Pushes all risk onto you. Avoid unless you're 100% certain about attendance.
Attrition above 30%: Hotel is protecting themselves too much. You should be able to negotiate better terms.
No attrition clause: Rare, but some courtesy blocks don't have attrition. This means you can walk away penalty-free
if the group doesn't fill.
CALCULATING ATTRITION RISK
Before accepting an attrition clause, calculate your worst-case scenario:
Example:
— 40-room block
— Room rate: $250/night
— 3-night stay
— 15% attrition (must fill 34 rooms)
Worst case: Only 30 rooms fill
— Shortfall: 4 rooms
— Penalty: 4 rooms × $250 × 3 nights = $3,000
Is your client (or you) willing to risk $3,000? If not, negotiate higher attrition.
ATTRITION AND CUT-OFF DATES
Attrition is typically calculated at the cut-off date, not at check-in.
Important: If your group hasn't reached the minimum by cut-off, you owe payment for the shortfall—even if the
hotel re-sells those rooms.
Some contracts allow you to adjust your block size down before cut-off without penalty. Always check this.
PROTECTING YOURSELF
1. Collect deposits from travelers early
Don't commit to a group block until you have confirmed interest and deposits from enough travelers to meet
attrition.
2. Set an internal cut-off earlier than hotel cut-off
If the hotel cut-off is 45 days out, set your client deadline at 55-60 days. This gives you time to adjust.
3. Negotiate sliding attrition
Some hotels will agree to adjust attrition based on how far out the event is:
— 12 months out: 20% attrition
— 6 months out: 15% attrition
— 3 months out: 10% attrition
This protects you early while locking in lower rates closer to the event.
4. Get attrition terms in writing
Make sure the contract clearly states:
— Attrition percentage
— How attrition is calculated (rooms? room nights? revenue?)
— When attrition is assessed (cut-off date? check-in?)
— Penalty structure
WHAT HAPPENS IF YOU DON'T MEET ATTRITION?
Scenario 1: You pay for unfilled rooms
Most common. You (or the client) pay the contracted rate for rooms that didn't fill.
Scenario 2: Attrition based on revenue, not rooms
Some contracts calculate attrition on total revenue. If your group upgrades to suites, you might meet revenue
attrition even with fewer rooms.
Scenario 3: Hotel resells rooms and waives penalty
Rare, but some contracts allow this. If the hotel re-sells your released rooms, they may waive the penalty. Don't
count on this.
COMMUNICATING ATTRITION TO CLIENTS
Always explain attrition clearly to clients:
> "We've secured a block of 30 rooms with 15% attrition. This means we need to fill at least 26 rooms by [cut-off
date]. If we don't reach 26 rooms, there will be a penalty of $[X] per unfilled room. Let's set a goal of 28 rooms to
give ourselves a buffer."
Transparency builds trust and ensures clients understand their responsibility.
COMMON QUESTIONS
Can I reduce my room block before cut-off?
Sometimes. Many contracts allow a one-time reduction (often 10-20% of original block) without penalty if done
60-90 days before arrival.
What if my group grows beyond the block size?
Hotels usually accommodate overflow at the group rate (subject to availability). Get this in writing.
Does attrition apply to comp rooms?
Usually no. Complimentary rooms typically don't count toward attrition calculations.
KEY TAKEAWAYS
— Aim for 15% attrition as a starting point for most groups
— Lower attrition = better rates, but higher risk
— Always calculate worst-case penalty before signing
— Collect traveler deposits early to reduce your risk
— Communicate attrition clearly to clients
RESOURCES
— Contract template with attrition clause examples: Portal
— Booking Platform team for complex negotiations

title: Group Booking Minimums by Supplier Type
body:
Published: November 22, 2025
Category: Group Bookings > Getting Started
OVERVIEW
Not all travel qualifies as a "group booking." Each supplier type has minimum thresholds that determine when group
rates and contracts apply. This guide breaks down what counts as a group across different travel categories.
HOTELS
Minimum: 10+ rooms per night
This is the industry standard across most hotel brands and properties.
Examples:
— Wedding with 12 hotel rooms = Group 
— Family reunion with 8 rooms = Not a group (book as FIT)
— Corporate retreat with 25 rooms = Group 
Important notes:
— 10 rooms must be needed on the same night (peak night)
— Multi-night stays: Group minimum applies to peak occupancy night
— Some boutique properties may accept groups at 8+ rooms, but 10 is standard
Major chain minimums:
— Marriott: 10 rooms
— Hilton: 10 rooms
— Hyatt: 10 rooms
— IHG: 10 rooms
— Four Seasons: 10 rooms
— Most independents: 10 rooms
CRUISES
Minimum: 8+ staterooms (cabins)
Cruise lines measure groups by staterooms/cabins, not passengers.
Examples:
— 8 cabins with 16 passengers = Group 
— 6 cabins with 24 passengers (families of 4) = Not a group
— 10 cabins = Group 
Important notes:
— Some cruise lines use 8 staterooms, others use 10
— River cruises often have lower minimums (5-6 cabins)
— Luxury lines may have different thresholds
Common minimums by line type:
— Mass market (Carnival, Royal Caribbean, NCL): 8 staterooms
— Premium (Celebrity, Princess, Holland America): 8 staterooms
— Luxury (Regent, Oceania, Silversea): 5-8 staterooms
— River cruises: 5-6 staterooms
TOURS
Minimum: 10+ travelers
Tour operators and wholesalers typically require 10 travelers minimum for group pricing.
Examples:
— 12 travelers on custom Italy trip = Group 
— 8 travelers on African safari = Not a group (but ask—some operators are flexible)
— 15 travelers on group tour = Group 
Important notes:
— Some tour operators offer group rates starting at 8 travelers
— Custom/private tours may have different minimums
— Destination and season affect minimums
OTHER TRAVEL TYPES
DMCs (Destination Management Companies)
Typical minimum: 10-15 travelers
DMCs provide on-ground services (transfers, guides, activities). Minimums vary by destination and services
requested.
All-Inclusive Resorts
Minimum: Often 10+ rooms (same as hotels)
Some all-inclusive brands offer group coordinators or perks starting at 8 rooms.
Villas
Minimum: No standard group minimum
Villa bookings are usually priced per property, not per room. Large villa bookings (multiple villas for one group) may
qualify for group discounts.
Group Air
Minimum: Typically 10+ passengers
Airlines offer group rates starting around 10 passengers on the same flight. Group air has its own rules and
contracts.
WHAT HAPPENS BELOW MINIMUMS?
If your client doesn't meet group minimums:
Book as individual (FIT) reservations:
— Use standard booking processes
— Each room/cabin booked separately
— No group contract
— May still request room blocks without formal contract (courtesy hold)
Courtesy holds:
— Some hotels offer informal room blocks below 10 rooms
— No contract required
— Holds are not guaranteed
— Useful for small weddings (5-8 rooms)
COMBINATION SCENARIOS
Multi-property groups:
If your group is splitting across multiple hotels, each property is evaluated separately.
Example:
— 6 rooms at Hotel A
— 5 rooms at Hotel B
— Neither property reaches 10-room minimum
— Book as FIT at both
Multi-night varying occupancy:
Group minimum applies to peak night.
Example:
— Friday: 12 rooms (peak)
— Saturday: 8 rooms
— Sunday: 6 rooms
— This qualifies as a group (12 rooms on peak night)
WHY MINIMUMS MATTER
Below minimum:
— No group contract
— No group coordinator/support
— No TC credits or GAP points (cruises)
— No complimentary rooms
— Individual cancellation policies apply
At or above minimum:
— Group contract with negotiated terms
— Dedicated group coordinator
— Potential for amenities and upgrades
— Group cancellation and attrition terms
— Commission on full group
EDGE CASES AND EXCEPTIONS
Ask the supplier when:
— Group is 1-2 rooms/cabins below minimum
— High-value group (luxury, extended stay)
— Repeat client or loyal advisor
— Shoulder season or low-demand period
Some suppliers will make exceptions, especially if the group has potential for growth or represents significant
revenue.
QUICK REFERENCE
| Supplier Type | Minimum | Unit |
|---------------|---------|------|
| Hotels | 10+ | Rooms |
| Cruises | 8+ | Staterooms |
| Tours | 10+ | Travelers |
| DMCs | 10-15 | Travelers |
| All-Inclusives | 10+ | Rooms |
| Group Air | 10+ | Passengers |
RESOURCES
— Group intake form: Collect details before reaching out to suppliers
— Supplier group contacts: Available in Fora Portal

title: Group Payment Collection Process
body:
Published: August 12, 2024
Category: Group Bookings > Operations
OVERVIEW
Managing payments for group bookings requires coordination between you, your client (the group organizer), and
individual travelers. This guide covers best practices for collecting and processing group payments.
PAYMENT STRUCTURE
Most group bookings follow a tiered payment schedule:
Hotels
Initial deposit: 20-30% at contract signing
Second payment: 60 days before arrival
Final payment: 30 days before arrival
Cruises
Initial deposit: $250-500 per person at booking
Final payment: 120 days before sailing (for most sailings over 6 nights)
Shorter cruises may have different timelines:
— 2-5 night cruises: 75 days before sailing
— 6+ night cruises: 120 days before sailing
Tours
Initial deposit: 25-30% at booking
Second payment: 90 days before departure
Final payment: 60 days before departure
TWO COLLECTION MODELS
Model 1: Advisor Collects from Travelers
You collect payments directly from each traveler and submit group payments to the supplier.
Pros:
— Full control over payment timing
— Direct relationship with travelers
— Can track who has/hasn't paid
Cons:
— Time-intensive
— You're responsible if travelers don't pay
— Need merchant account for credit card processing
Best for: Small groups (10-20 travelers), advisors with established payment processing
Model 2: Group Organizer Collects Payments
The group organizer (client) collects money from travelers and sends you lump sum payments.
Pros:
— Less administrative work for you
— Group organizer is responsible for collecting
— Only deal with one point of contact
Cons:
— Less visibility into who has paid
— Dependent on organizer's follow-through
— May be delays if organizer is slow to collect
Best for: Larger groups, corporate groups, weddings where couple handles guests
RECOMMENDED TIMELINE (HOTELS)
12+ months before event:
— Contract signed
— Initial deposit due (20-30%)
6 months before:
— Begin collecting traveler deposits
— Rooming list takes shape
90 days before:
— Second deposit due to hotel (if applicable)
60 days before:
— Push for final traveler commitments
— Cut-off date approaching
45 days before:
— Cut-off date (release unused rooms)
— Final payment due to hotel
30 days before:
— Finalize rooming list
— Submit special requests
RECOMMENDED TIMELINE (CRUISES)
12+ months before sailing:
— Book group (contracted or spec)
— Collect initial deposits ($250-500/person)
6 months before:
— Promote sailing
— Continue collecting bookings
120 days before (final payment):
— Collect final payments from travelers
— Submit to cruise line
90 days before:
— Finalize cabin assignments
— Submit dietary restrictions, special requests
60 days before:
— Work with group coordinator on TC Credits and GAP Points
PAYMENT METHODS
Credit Cards
Pros: Instant payment, secure, traveler protection
Cons: Processing fees (2-3%), may hit credit limits on large groups
Best for: Individual traveler payments
ACH/Bank Transfer
Pros: Lower fees, good for large payments
Cons: Slower processing (3-5 days), requires bank account info
Best for: Group organizer lump sum payments, final payments
Checks
Pros: No processing fees
Cons: Slow, risk of bounced checks, outdated
Best for: Older clients, smaller deposits
Wire Transfer
Pros: Fast, secure, good for international
Cons: High fees ($25-50), requires bank info
Best for: International groups, very large payments
HANDLING LATE PAYMENTS
Set internal deadlines earlier than supplier deadlines
If hotel wants final payment 30 days out, tell clients it's due 40 days out. This gives you buffer.
Send reminder emails at key milestones:
— 60 days before: "Second payment due in 2 weeks"
— 45 days before: "Final payment due soon"
— 35 days before: "Final reminder—payment due in 5 days"
Have a plan for non-payment:
— Can you cover shortfall temporarily?
— Will you charge late fees?
— At what point do you cancel the traveler's reservation?
REFUND AND CANCELLATION POLICIES
Hotels
Cancellation penalties vary by contract. Typical structure:
— 90+ days out: Refund minus deposit
— 60-90 days: 50% penalty
— 30-60 days: 75% penalty
— 0-30 days: No refund
Always communicate cancellation terms clearly when collecting payments.
Cruises
Cruise lines have specific cancellation schedules based on sailing length and departure date. Generally:
— 120+ days out: Refund minus deposit ($100-250/person)
— 90-119 days: 50% penalty
— 60-89 days: 75% penalty
— 0-59 days: 100% penalty (no refund)
Important: Cruise cancellation policies are stricter than hotel policies.
MANAGING GROUP FINANCES
Use a spreadsheet to track:
— Traveler names
— Payment amounts and dates
— Balance owed
— Cancellation requests
Separate business and personal finances:
— Use a dedicated business account for group payments
— Don't mingle group funds with personal money
Track supplier payment deadlines:
— Set reminders for deposit and final payment dates
— Don't miss deadlines (penalties can apply)
DEPOSITS VS. FINAL PAYMENTS
Deposits:
— Typically non-refundable or partially refundable
— Lock in rates and hold inventory
— Usually 20-30% of total
Final payments:
— Due 30-120 days before travel (depending on supplier)
— Remaining balance after deposits
— Often non-refundable once submitted
Make sure travelers understand the difference. Many assume deposits are fully refundable.
PAYMENT DISPUTES
What to do if a traveler disputes a charge:
1. Review your payment terms (did you communicate policies clearly?)
2. Check the supplier's cancellation policy
3. Try to work out a compromise (credit toward future travel?)
4. Escalate to Booking Platform team if needed
Protect yourself:
— Send written confirmation of all payments
— Keep records of cancellation policy acknowledgments
— Use contracts for larger groups
TOOLS TO STREAMLINE PAYMENTS
Payment processing platforms:
— Stripe
— PayPal
— Square
Group travel payment platforms:
— WeTravel
— Travefy
— TravelJoy
These platforms help you:
— Collect payments online
— Send automated reminders
— Track who has/hasn't paid
— Handle refunds
RED FLAGS
Watch out for:
— Group organizer who's slow to collect payments (puts you at risk)
— Travelers asking to pay "later" repeatedly
— Last-minute cancellations after final payment deadline
— Requests to bypass payment terms
If payments aren't coming in on schedule, escalate with the group organizer early.
WHEN TO ESCALATE TO BOOKING PLATFORM
Contact BP team for:
— Complex payment disputes
— International payment questions
— Large group payments (50+ travelers)
— Supplier payment issues
KEY TAKEAWAYS
— Always set internal deadlines earlier than supplier deadlines
— Choose collection model based on group size and organizer reliability
— Track payments carefully with spreadsheets or tools
— Communicate cancellation policies clearly upfront
— Use secure payment methods (credit cards, ACH)
RESOURCES
— Payment tracking template: Available in Portal
— Traveler payment agreement template: Available in Portal

title: Cruise Group Booking Types Explained
body:
Published: December 18, 2025
Category: Group Bookings > Cruises
OVERVIEW
Cruise group bookings work differently than hotel groups. There are three main booking types, each with different
requirements, flexibility, and benefits. Understanding when to use each type is key to serving your clients
effectively.
GROUP MINIMUMS
Minimum for cruise groups: 8+ staterooms
Group sizes:
— Small groups: 8-15 staterooms
— Medium groups: 15-50 staterooms
— Large groups: 50-250+ staterooms
— Buyouts/Charters: Full or partial ship
THREE BOOKING TYPES
1. Speculative Hold (Spec)
A non-committal hold on cabins while you gauge interest from your group.
Key features:
— No initial deposit required
— Flexible hold on inventory
— Can release cabins without penalty
— Recall date: Cruise line can recall cabins with notice (typically 30-90 days before sailing)
Best for:
— Testing interest in a sailing
— Groups still forming
— Uncertain final headcount
— New advisors building group travel business
How it works:
1. Request spec hold from cruise line group desk
2. Promote sailing to your client base
3. As individuals commit, book them into the block
4. Before recall date, either convert to contracted or release
Important: Spec holds give cruise line flexibility to recall inventory if the ship starts filling. Don't wait too long to
convert.
2. Contracted Group
A formal commitment with deposit and minimum cabin requirements.
Key features:
— Deposit required (typically 10-20% of total)
— Minimum cabin commitment (varies by cruise line)
— Attrition clause (similar to hotel groups)
— Better pricing and amenities than spec
— Locked-in rates
Best for:
— Committed groups with confirmed travelers
— Larger groups (15+ cabins)
— Peak sailing dates
— Groups wanting best rates and perks
How it works:
1. Sign group contract with cruise line
2. Submit initial deposit
3. Promote sailing and collect traveler deposits
4. Meet minimum cabin requirement by cut-off date
5. Pay for unfilled cabins if below attrition threshold
Example:
— Contract for 20 cabins
— 20% attrition (must fill 16 cabins)
— If only 14 cabins fill, group pays for 2 additional cabins
3. Charter (Full or Partial)
Exclusive use of an entire ship or large section.
Key features:
— Buyout of ship or significant portion (100+ cabins)
— Custom itineraries possible
— Exclusive events and branding
— Significant upfront commitment
— Highest level of customization
Best for:
— Large corporate incentive groups
— Destination weddings with 100+ guests
— Conferences or conventions
— Ultra-luxury private experiences
How it works:
1. Submit charter request to cruise line
2. Negotiate custom terms (itinerary, pricing, F&B, entertainment)
3. Substantial deposit (often 25%+ of total)
4. Work with cruise line event planning team
5. Coordinate all logistics directly
CHOOSING THE RIGHT BOOKING TYPE
| Scenario | Recommended Type |
|----------|------------------|
| Testing interest for a future sailing | Speculative |
| 8-12 cabins, casual group | Speculative |
| 15+ cabins, committed travelers | Contracted |
| Peak season sailing (limited inventory) | Contracted |
| Want best pricing and amenities | Contracted |
| 100+ cabins, exclusive experience | Charter |
| Custom itinerary needed | Charter |
CONVERTING FROM SPEC TO CONTRACTED
Many groups start as spec and convert to contracted once interest is confirmed.
When to convert:
— Group size justifies better contracted rates
— Recall date approaching
— You've reached 60-70% of target cabin count
— Sailing is filling and you want to lock inventory
Process:
1. Contact cruise line group desk
2. Negotiate contracted terms
3. Submit deposit
4. Existing spec bookings transfer to contracted block
GROUP BENEFITS: TC CREDITS AND GAP POINTS
Regardless of booking type, groups earn special perks:
Tour Conductor (TC) Credits:
— Earned at rate of approximately 1 TC credit per 8-10 cabins (varies by cruise line)
— Can be redeemed for: free cabin for group leader, onboard events, or additional commission
Group Amenity Points (GAP):
— Points awarded based on group size
— Redeemable for: onboard credit, welcome gifts, happy hours, specialty dining, shore excursions, Wi-Fi
packages
TC Credits and GAP details vary by cruise line. Check with each line's group desk for specifics.
IMPORTANT NOTES
Cabins vs. Berths:
— Cruise lines measure groups in staterooms/cabins, not passengers
— A cabin with 2 passengers = 1 cabin toward group minimum
— A cabin with 4 passengers = still 1 cabin (not 2)
Recall Dates:
— Spec holds have recall dates when cruise line can take back inventory
— Contracted groups also have final payment and cut-off dates
— Missing these dates can result in lost inventory or penalties
Individual Names:
— Spec bookings: Log individually as travelers commit
— Contracted: May hold cabins without names initially, add names before final payment
WHEN TO ESCALATE
Contact Booking Platform team for:
— Charter requests
— Multi-ship or back-to-back sailing groups
— Groups over 50 cabins
— Custom itinerary requests
— Contract negotiation support
— Issues with cruise line group desks
RESOURCES
— Cruise line group desk contacts: Available in Fora Portal
— Group sales minimum requirements by cruise line

title: TC Credits and GAP Points: What Advisors Need to Know
body:
Published: October 15, 2025
Category: Group Bookings > Cruises
OVERVIEW
Cruise groups earn special perks beyond standard commission: Tour Conductor (TC) Credits and Group Amenity
Points (GAP). Understanding how these work helps you maximize value for your clients and yourself.
TOUR CONDUCTOR (TC) CREDITS
What are TC Credits?
TC Credits are earned based on your group size. Think of them as rewards from the cruise line for bringing group
business.
How many do you earn?
Earning rate: Approximately 1 TC credit per 8-10 cabins booked
The exact rate varies by cruise line:
— Mass market lines (Carnival, Royal Caribbean, NCL): 1 per 8 cabins
— Premium lines (Celebrity, Princess): 1 per 8-10 cabins
— Luxury lines (Regent, Oceania, Silversea): 1 per 5-8 cabins
Example:
— 20-cabin group on Royal Caribbean = 2-3 TC credits
— 40-cabin group on Celebrity = 4-5 TC credits
How can TC Credits be used?
TC Credits can be redeemed for:
1. Free cabin for group leader/tour conductor
— Most common use
— Group organizer travels free
2. Onboard events or amenities
— Private cocktail party
— Specialty dining for the group
— Shore excursions
— Onboard credit distributed to travelers
3. Additional commission
— Convert TC credit to extra advisor commission
— Amount varies by cruise line
Important notes:
— TC Credits must be used on the same sailing (cannot be banked for future cruises)
— One TC credit typically equals one free inside cabin; upgrades available for additional cost
— Credits are awarded when group meets minimum cabin threshold
— Advisor decides how to allocate TC credits
GROUP AMENITY POINTS (GAP)
What are GAP Points?
GAP Points are another cruise line perk awarded to groups. These points can be exchanged for various amenities
to enhance the group experience.
How many do you earn?
GAP Points are awarded based on total cabins booked. The formula varies by cruise line.
Typical structure:
— 10 cabins = 50 GAP points
— 20 cabins = 120 GAP points
— 40 cabins = 300 GAP points
Larger groups earn points at a better rate (economies of scale).
What can GAP Points be redeemed for?
Onboard credit (OBC):
— Applied to travelers' shipboard accounts
— Can be split among group or given to specific cabins
Welcome amenities:
— Champagne, chocolate-covered strawberries, or welcome gifts in cabins
Group events:
— Happy hours
— Private parties
— Themed events
Wi-Fi packages:
— Internet access for group members
Specialty dining:
— Group dinners at specialty restaurants
Shore excursions:
— Discounted or complimentary group excursions
Beverage packages or spa services:
— Some cruise lines allow GAP redemption for these
How to use GAP Points:
Work with the cruise line's group coordinator to select amenities. Redemption happens before sailing (typically
30-60 days out).
TC CREDITS VS. GAP POINTS: WHAT'S THE DIFFERENCE?
| Feature | TC Credits | GAP Points |
|---------|------------|------------|
| Earned based on | Cabins booked | Cabins booked |
| Redemption | Free cabin, commission, or event | Amenities, OBC, events |
| Flexibility | Limited redemption options | Many redemption options |
| Who benefits | Group leader or advisor | Entire group |
| Typical use | Free cabin for organizer | OBC or welcome gifts for travelers |
STRATEGIC USE OF TC CREDITS AND GAP POINTS
Scenario 1: Affinity Group (Travel Club)
— TC Credits: Use for free cabin for club president
— GAP Points: Distribute as onboard credit to all members ($50 per cabin)
Scenario 2: Incentive Trip (Corporate)
— TC Credits: Convert to additional commission (company paid for all travel)
— GAP Points: Private cocktail party and specialty dining event
Scenario 3: Family Reunion
— TC Credits: Free cabin for family organizer (grandparents)
— GAP Points: Welcome amenities in all cabins + group shore excursion
Scenario 4: Milestone Celebration (50th Birthday)
— TC Credits: Free cabin for birthday honoree
— GAP Points: Private party, OBC for guests, champagne in cabins
WHEN ARE TC CREDITS AND GAP POINTS AWARDED?
— Contracted groups: Awarded once group meets minimum cabin commitment
— Speculative groups: Awarded as cabins are booked (may increase as group grows)
— Final calculation: Determined at final payment (90 days before sailing)
If your group shrinks below minimum after final payment, TC Credits and GAP may be reduced.
WORKING WITH CRUISE LINE GROUP COORDINATORS
Your group coordinator will help you:
— Calculate TC Credits and GAP Points
— Choose redemption options
— Coordinate onboard events
— Manage changes to group size
Contact your coordinator 60-90 days before sailing to finalize TC and GAP usage.
COMMON QUESTIONS
Can I split TC Credits?
No. Each TC credit equals one cabin. You cannot split a credit across multiple cabins.
Can I save GAP Points for another cruise?
No. TC Credits and GAP Points must be used on the sailing for which they were earned.
What if my group grows after I've redeemed GAP Points?
Contact your group coordinator. Additional points may be awarded, and you can adjust redemptions.
Do river cruises offer TC Credits and GAP?
Some do, but terms vary widely. Check with each river cruise line.
IMPORTANT REMINDERS
— Always factor TC Credits and GAP into your group value proposition
— Explain these benefits to clients when pitching group travel
— Work closely with cruise line group coordinators to maximize value
— Redemption deadlines vary by cruise line—don't miss them
RESOURCES
— Cruise line group desk contacts: Available in Fora Portal
— Group coordinator assignment: Provided when group is contracted

title: Commission Structures for Group Travel
body:
Published: June 8, 2023
Category: Group Bookings > Commissions
OVERVIEW
Group bookings earn commission based on the total value of the group. Understanding commission structures
helps you price appropriately and set client expectations.
HOTEL GROUP COMMISSIONS
Major chains (Marriott, Hilton, Hyatt, IHG): 8-10%
Boutique and luxury hotels: 10-15%
All-inclusive resorts: 10-12%
How hotel commission is calculated:
Commission is typically calculated on:
— Room rate × number of room nights
— Does NOT include: taxes, resort fees, parking, F&B
Example:
— 20 rooms × 3 nights = 60 room nights
— Average rate: $200/night
— Total room revenue: $12,000
— Commission at 10%: $1,200
When is commission paid?
Most hotels pay commission 30-60 days after group checkout.
CRUISE GROUP COMMISSIONS
Standard commission: 15-16%
Luxury cruise lines: 18-20%
River cruises: 15-18%
How cruise commission is calculated:
Commission is calculated on:
— Base fare (per person)
— Port charges and taxes (sometimes included, sometimes not—check with cruise line)
— Does NOT include: Gratuities, shore excursions, specialty dining, beverages
Example:
— 20 cabins × 2 passengers = 40 passengers
— Average fare: $2,000/person
— Total cruise revenue: $80,000
— Commission at 16%: $12,800
Tiered commission structures:
Some cruise lines offer higher commission for larger groups:
— 8-15 cabins: 16%
— 16-30 cabins: 17%
— 31+ cabins: 18%
When is commission paid?
Cruise lines typically pay commission after final payment (90 days before sailing) or after sailing, depending on the
line.
TOUR OPERATOR COMMISSIONS
Standard tour operators: 15-18%
Luxury/custom tours: 18-20%
Adventure tour operators: 10-15%
Commission is calculated on the land package price (accommodations, transportation, activities). Flights are
usually booked separately.
DMC (DESTINATION MANAGEMENT COMPANY) COMMISSIONS
Typical commission: 10-15%
DMCs provide on-ground services. Commission varies based on:
— Destination
— Services provided
— Volume of business
WHOLESALER COMMISSIONS
Typical commission: 10-12%
Wholesalers package hotels + tours + transfers. Commission is lower because they're aggregating services.
GROUP AIR COMMISSIONS
Group air bookings typically earn minimal commission from airlines (if any). Instead:
— Charge a service fee to the client
— Work with consolidators who offer net rates + markup
Most advisors charge $50-100 per ticket for group air coordination.
SPECIAL PROGRAMS AND OVERRIDES
Many suppliers offer bonus commission for high-performing advisors:
Virtuoso groups: May earn additional commission or amenities
Preferred suppliers: Often offer 1-2% commission override
Volume bonuses: Book 100+ rooms/year with a chain  earn extra commission
COMMISSION PAYMENT TIMELINES
| Supplier Type | Typical Payment Timing |
|---------------|------------------------|
| Hotels | 30-60 days after checkout |
| Cruises | 90 days pre-sailing or post-sailing |
| Tour operators | 30-45 days after trip start |
| DMCs | 30 days after services rendered |
| All-inclusives | 60-90 days after checkout |
PROTECTING YOUR COMMISSION
Always:
— Register the group with the supplier
— Get commission agreement in writing
— Track group bookings in your CRM
— Follow up if payment is late
Watch out for:
— Clients booking direct after you've done the work (send confirmation emails establishing your role)
— Suppliers claiming group didn't meet minimum (document final numbers)
— Commission clawbacks if group cancels (understand cancellation commission policies)
COMMISSION VS. SERVICE FEES
Some advisors charge clients a service fee on top of commission for:
— Complex multi-destination groups
— Groups requiring significant coordination
— Groups with custom requests
Typical service fees: $50-150 per person or 5-10% of total trip cost
IMPORTANT NOTES
— Commission structures can vary by season and demand
— Always confirm commission rate before signing group contract
— Some suppliers pay commission on a net rate (you markup), others on gross rate
— International groups may have different commission structures
WHEN TO ASK FOR HIGHER COMMISSION
You may be able to negotiate higher commission for:
— Very large groups (50+ rooms)
— Multi-property or multi-year agreements
— Groups during low season
— Repeat clients
RESOURCES
— Supplier commission schedules: Available in Portal
— Commission tracking: Log groups in Fora CRM
NEED HELP?
Contact Booking Platform team if you have questions about:
— Unusual commission structures
— Delayed commission payments
— Negotiating commission overrides

title: Working with Tour Operators for Groups
body:
Published: February 22, 2023
Category: Group Bookings > Tours
OVERVIEW
Tour operators and DMCs (Destination Management Companies) are valuable partners for group travel, especially
when you need local expertise or pre-packaged itineraries. This guide covers when and how to work with them.
WHAT IS A TOUR OPERATOR?
A tour operator creates and sells packaged travel experiences. They handle:
— Accommodations
— Transportation
— Activities and excursions
— Guides
— Some meals
Tour operators work with multiple suppliers to build complete itineraries. You book the package, and they handle
logistics.
WHAT IS A DMC?
A DMC (Destination Management Company) provides on-the-ground services in a specific destination. They offer:
— Airport transfers
— Local guides
— Activity coordination
— Restaurant reservations
— Custom experiences
DMCs are local experts who help you execute itineraries, but they don't usually create full packages like tour
operators do.
TOUR OPERATOR VS. DMC: QUICK COMPARISON
| Feature | Tour Operator | DMC |
|---------|---------------|-----|
| Scope | Full packages | On-ground services |
| Geography | Global or regional | Specific destination |
| Customization | Limited | Highly customizable |
| Cost | Fixed package pricing | A la carte pricing |
| Best for | Larger groups, set itineraries | Custom trips, local support |
WHEN TO USE A TOUR OPERATOR
Best for:
— Groups that want a pre-built itinerary
— First-time group organizers
— Budget-conscious groups (packages can be cost-effective)
— Popular destinations with established tours (Europe, Southeast Asia, etc.)
Examples:
— 15-person group tour of Italy with fixed itinerary
— Educational group trip to Costa Rica
— Multi-generational family trip to Ireland
Pros:
— Simplified planning (package already designed)
— Competitive pricing (economies of scale)
— Experienced tour managers
— Established supplier relationships
Cons:
— Less flexibility
— Set departure dates
— May include travelers outside your group
WHEN TO USE A DMC
Best for:
— Custom itineraries
— VIP or luxury groups
— Corporate incentive trips
— Groups needing local expertise in complex destinations
Examples:
— Custom Japan itinerary for 12 travelers
— Incentive trip in South Africa with unique activities
— Destination wedding in Mexico with local coordination
Pros:
— Fully customizable
— Local expertise
— High-touch service
— Works with your dates and preferences
Cons:
— More expensive than packaged tours
— Requires more planning on your part
— May need to coordinate multiple vendors
FINDING THE RIGHT PARTNER
For tour operators:
— Check if they specialize in your destination
— Ask about group minimums (usually 10+ travelers)
— Review sample itineraries
— Verify they're accredited (USTOA, ASTA, etc.)
For DMCs:
— Confirm they operate in your destination
— Ask for references from other advisors
— Understand their service area and capabilities
— Check response time (good DMCs respond quickly)
COMMISSION STRUCTURES
Tour operators: 15-18% commission on land packages
DMCs: 10-15% commission on services booked
Commission is usually paid after travel is complete (30-60 days post-trip).
WORKING WITH TOUR OPERATORS: BEST PRACTICES
1. Request group quotes early
Tour operators need lead time (3-6 months minimum for custom requests).
2. Understand what's included
Packages vary. Clarify:
— Accommodations (room types, locations)
— Meals (how many, what's included)
— Transportation (private coach, train, flights)
— Activities (entrance fees, guided tours)
— Gratuities (included or extra)
3. Review cancellation policies
Tour packages often have strict cancellation terms:
— 90+ days: Partial refund
— 60-90 days: 50% penalty
— 30-60 days: 75% penalty
— 0-30 days: No refund
4. Confirm group minimums
Most tour operators require 10+ travelers. If your group is smaller, ask if they can accommodate.
5. Negotiate when possible
Larger groups (20+) may have room to negotiate pricing, upgrades, or included amenities.
WORKING WITH DMCS: BEST PRACTICES
1. Provide detailed briefs
DMCs need to understand:
— Group size and demographics
— Travel dates
— Budget
— Interests and preferences
— Any special requirements
2. Ask for multiple options
Request 2-3 proposals at different price points.
3. Build relationships
Good DMCs become long-term partners. Treat them well, communicate clearly, and they'll prioritize your groups.
4. Confirm services in writing
Everything should be documented: dates, times, services, pricing.
5. Coordinate timing
DMCs work in local time zones. Factor in time differences for communication.
RED FLAGS
Watch out for:
— Tour operators or DMCs with no online presence or reviews
— Pricing that seems too good to be true
— Poor communication or slow responses
— No clear cancellation or refund policies
— Requests for full payment far in advance
COMMON MISTAKES
1. Booking too close to travel dates
Tour operators and DMCs need lead time. Last-minute requests are difficult and expensive.
2. Not reading the fine print
Understand what's included and what costs extra.
3. Assuming all tour operators are the same
Quality varies. Vet partners carefully.
4. Forgetting about visas and entry requirements
Tour operators usually don't handle visas. You're responsible for advising travelers on entry requirements.
GROUP MINIMUMS FOR TOUR OPERATORS
Most tour operators require:
— Minimum: 10 travelers
— Ideal group size: 15-25 travelers
— Large groups: 30+ travelers may qualify for private departures or custom terms
Smaller groups (8-10) may be accommodated but at higher per-person pricing.
PAYMENT TERMS
Tour operators:
— Deposit: 25-30% at booking
— Final payment: 60-90 days before departure
DMCs:
— Deposit: 30-50% at booking
— Final payment: 30 days before services rendered
WHEN TO ESCALATE
Contact Booking Platform or Tour Ops team for:
— Complex multi-destination groups
— Groups over 30 travelers
— Issues with tour operator or DMC performance
— Contract negotiation support
RESOURCES
— Preferred tour operator list: Available in Portal
— DMC contact database: Available in Portal
KEY TAKEAWAYS
— Use tour operators for pre-packaged, budget-friendly group travel
— Use DMCs for custom, high-touch experiences in specific destinations
— Vet partners carefully and build long-term relationships
— Understand what's included and communicate clearly with clients
— Allow plenty of lead time for group travel planning
Confidential Knowledge Base · 44
